stories.title: Self-Storage Developer Closes On Loan for 1,500-Unit Expansion
stories.excerpt: UTEX Storage Partners closed on a $42.5 million construction loan to finance a two-property self-storage portfolio in the Miami metropolitan area. Affinius Capital and Axonic Capital originated the loan. The financing was arranged by Daniel Hartnett of Greysteel. The first property, located in Pembroke Pines, FL, is a to-be-built, four-story facility which will offer 1,097 units. The second, located in Coral Gables, FL, involves the 540-unit expansion of an existing self-storage facility, which will total 1,500 units upon completion.  Upon completion, the portfolio will total 2,597 units across two properties: a four-story, 100,125-square-foot facility with 1,097 units in Pembroke Pines; and two three-story buildings totaling 132,995 square feet and 1,500 units in Coral Gables. All spaces will be fully climate-controlled with interior and drive-up access and modern loading features for enhanced accessibility. The post Self-Storage Developer Closes On Loan for 1,500-Unit Expansion appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: UTEX Storage closed $42.5M construction loan for 1,500-unit self-storage expansion in Miami area.
Pembroke Pines 4-story, 1,097-unit facility and Coral Gables 1,500-unit expansion totaling 2,597 units.
Self-storage supply surge in Miami metro signals strong logistics/warehousing demand tied to coastal e-commerce hubs.
stories.summary: UTEX Storage Partners secured $42.5M in construction financing from Affinius Capital and Axonic Capital to build a 1,097-unit self-storage facility in Pembroke Pines and expand an existing property in Coral Gables by 540 units, for a combined 2,597-unit portfolio. Both properties will feature climate-controlled, fully-accessible spaces with interior and drive-up access. The financing arrangement underscores strong capital availability for storage assets in South Florida.
stories.impact: Self-storage expansion in Miami reflects logistics-driven demand from coastal e-commerce and population density, not residential rental fundamentals. Minimal direct impact on multifamily development, though it signals capital competition and land-use preferences in Miami submarket during a period of constrained multifamily financing.

stories.title: Berkadia Arranges Sale, Financing of 270-Unit VA Multifamily Property
stories.excerpt: Berkadia has arranged the sale and financing of Masons Keepe, a 270-unit garden-style multifamily community located in Manassas, Virginia.Senior Managing Director Brian Crivella, Managing Directors Bill Gribbin and Yalda Ghamarian, and Associate Director Jack Canepa of Berkadia DC Metro, along with Senior Managing Director Drew White of Berkadia Richmond, arranged the sale on behalf of University Village Apartments, LLC. Senior Managing Director Brad Williamson of Berkadia Miami and Senior Managing Director Christopher Ellis of Berkadia Denver secured financing on behalf of the buyer, Bridge Investment Group. Berkadia originated, and Freddie Mac secured, the five-year, fixed-rate loan with a three-year interest-only period, a 35-year amortization schedule, and a 70% loan-to-value ratio.Located at 9000 Landings Drive, Masons Keepe was built in 2004 and features a mix of one- and two-bedroom apartment homes. The property provides convenient access to employment centers throughout Prince William County and the greater Washington, D.C. metropolitan area. If you’re reading about the multifamily industry, you should be in the room with its leaders. Connect Apartments brings together the executives featured in Connect CRE’s coverage for an afternoon of high-level insights and unmatched networking on September 22 at The Biltmore in LA. The post Berkadia Arranges Sale, Financing of 270-Unit VA Multifamily Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Bridge Investment Group acquired 270-unit Masons Keepe garden-style community in Manassas, VA via Berkadia.
Freddie Mac secured 5-year fixed, 70% LTV loan with 3-year IO period and 35-year amortization.
2004-vintage asset sale signals institutional appetite for Virginia suburban multifamily near DC employment centers.
stories.summary: Berkadia arranged the sale of Masons Keepe, a 270-unit garden-style apartment community built in 2004 in Manassas, Virginia, to Bridge Investment Group. Freddie Mac provided a 5-year fixed-rate loan at 70% LTV with a 3-year interest-only period and 35-year amortization. The property provides access to Prince William County and greater DC employment hubs.
stories.impact: Bridge's acquisition of a 22-year-old Virginia suburban asset signals institutional confidence in DC-adjacent multifamily NOI despite higher rates. The 70% LTV and IO structure indicate lender comfort with workforce housing in job-rich submarkets; implies continued capital flow into Northern Virginia/Manassas for value-add and stabilized plays, supporting acquisition prices for developers looking to sell or refinance in this submarket.

stories.title: HarbourVest Will Open First NYC Office at 9 West 57th
stories.excerpt: JLL represented global private markets investment firm HarbourVest Partners in securing its first New York City office at Soloviev Group’s 9 W. 57th St. The 10-year lease is for 19,752 square feet on a portion of the building’s 49th floor. Headquartered in Boston, HarbourVest is among the world’s leading private markets investment firms, managing more than $160 billion in assets. The firm’s New York office marks a milestone in its continued growth. JLL vice chairman Alexander Chudnoff and associate Ava Loughlin represented HarbourVest. Ownership was represented by CBRE’s Howard Fiddle, John Maher, Gregg Rothkin and Alex Leopold. “HarbourVest wanted its office in New York City to reflect the caliber of its business, and to provide an exceptional experience for both clients and employees,” said Chudnoff. “Few buildings deliver that combination better than 9 W. 57th St., which continues to attract many of the world’s most prestigious financial and investment firms.” The post HarbourVest Will Open First NYC Office at 9 West 57th appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: HarbourVest Partners secured first NYC office; 19,752 SF lease at 9 W. 57th St. for 10 years.
$160B AUM firm joins financial services migration into Midtown office; reflects capital manager consolidation in NYC.
No direct multifamily relevance; office leasing trend in trophy Midtown towers unrelated to residential demand.
stories.summary: HarbourVest Partners, a Boston-headquartered private markets investment firm managing $160B in assets, leased 19,752 square feet on the 49th floor of 9 West 57th Street in Midtown Manhattan for 10 years. The move marks HarbourVest's first New York office and reflects the firm's growth and desire for a prestigious Midtown location alongside other leading financial and investment firms.
stories.impact: Office leasing activity in trophy Midtown towers has no direct bearing on multifamily demand, supply, or capital. This is a corporate real estate event in the commercial office market, not a signal of residential demand drivers.

stories.title: CIM Group, Hulic Acquire West Hollywood Apartment Property
stories.excerpt: CIM Group completed the acquisition of Domain WeHo, a 166-unit apartment community in West Hollywood, in partnership with Hulic Co., Ltd. Although terms were not disclosed, LA Business First reported that the previous owner, RREEF Property Trust, paid more than $103 million in 2019. Domain is a seven-story building with 9,185 square feet of ground-floor retail located at 7141 Santa Monica Blvd. west of La Brea Avenue, a major business corridor and active neighborhood with several restaurants, retail and entertainment venues as well as creative offices. Opened in fall 2017, Domain offers studio, one- and two-bedroom residences, including 33 designated as affordable. According to LA Business First, Domain WeHo sits kitty-corner to the studio campus The Lot at Formosa, also owned by CIM Group. Last year, the firm transferred ownership of the property to a shell company controlled by CIM as part of a recapitalization. The conversations behind tomorrow’s apartment deals happen at Connect Apartments on September 22 in LA. Connect with the capital sources, owners, developers, and brokers driving multifamily investment. Learn more and reserve your seat at www.connectapts2026.com. The post CIM Group, Hulic Acquire West Hollywood Apartment Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CIM Group and Hulic Co. acquired Domain WeHo, 166-unit apartment community in West Hollywood.
Previous owner RREEF paid $103M in 2019; CIM holds adjacent studio campus; 33 units designated affordable.
West Hollywood portfolio concentration and mixed-use adjacency signal CIM's value-add strategy in tight LA submarket.
stories.summary: CIM Group, in partnership with Hulic Co., Ltd., acquired Domain WeHo, a 166-unit apartment community on Santa Monica Boulevard in West Hollywood that opened in fall 2017. The building includes 33 affordable units and 9,185 SF of ground-floor retail. The previous owner, RREEF Property Trust, had paid more than $103M for the property in 2019. CIM already owns the adjacent Lot at Formosa studio campus.
stories.impact: CIM's acquisition of Domain WeHo adjacent to its owned studio campus signals a landlord strategy to consolidate mixed-use entertainment/creative office and residential assets in West Hollywood. This suggests CIM believes synergies exist between creative office tenants and the residential product, and that premium rents in West Hollywood remain defensible. The transaction indicates continued institutional appetite for West LA multifamily despite higher rates, supporting land values and development feasibility in the Santa Monica Blvd corridor for new product.

stories.title: Retail Veterans Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy Join JLL
stories.excerpt: There are some new recruits at JLL, the brokerage firm announced Monday. Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy, who co-founded retail and entertainment real estate firm Dreamscape, have joined JLL as executive managing director and senior vice president, respectively, to help further drive the firm’s experiential retail, entertainment and mixed-use leasing initiatives. “Josh and Scott have built exceptional careers helping owners and brands create compelling destinations that resonate with today’s consumers,” Patrick A. Smith, vice chairman at JLL, said in a statement. “Their relationships, creativity and deep understanding of the retail landscape will be a tremendous asset to our clients and further enhance the capabilities of both our New York and national project leasing platforms.” The pair have advised on some of the most notable mixed-use and entertainment leases in the country over the last 10 years, including deals at the South Street Seaport in New York City, retail marketplace the Arcade in Nashville, and shopping center Bayside Marketplace in Miami. “Scott and I are excited to join JLL at a time when retail continues to evolve across both urban and suburban markets,” Strauss said in a statement. “While experiential retail has become an increasingly important driver of consumer demand, we remain deeply focused on helping landlords and tenants navigate every aspect of the retail landscape.” Before joining Dreamscape, Strauss and Zinovoy also worked together at independent real estate firm RKF and Newmark, which acquired RKF in 2018. “We were drawn to JLL because of its industry-leading platform, global reach, outstanding reputation and, most importantly, its people and collaborative culture,” Zinovoy said in a statement. “JLL provides the ideal environment to continue growing our business while helping clients bring transformative visions to life.” Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy (Dreamscape co-founders) joined JLL as experiential retail and mixed-use leaders.
Pair advised South Street Seaport, Arcade Nashville, Bayside Miami—flagship entertainment/mixed-use destinations.
Recruitment signals JLL's expansion of mixed-use brokerage; no direct multifamily supply/demand signal.
stories.summary: JLL announced the hiring of Joshua Strauss as executive managing director and Scott Zinovoy as senior vice president to lead the firm's experiential retail, entertainment, and mixed-use leasing initiatives. Both are co-founders of Dreamscape and have advised on major mixed-use and entertainment leases including South Street Seaport in NYC, the Arcade in Nashville, and Bayside Marketplace in Miami over the past decade.
stories.impact: Recruitment of top retail/mixed-use specialists to JLL underscores growing capital focus on entertainment-anchored mixed-use development. Indirectly relevant to multifamily: JLL's deeper mixed-use expertise signals developer demand for integrated retail-residential product, which may support acquisition premiums and development economics for mixed-use assets in urban/entertainment districts; minimal impact on pure multifamily development.

stories.title: Infinium Wall Systems Takes 30K SF of Office, Retail Space at ESRT’s 1359 Broadway
stories.excerpt: If these walls could talk, they’d talk about all the new leasing in Midtown. Infinium Wall Systems, a company that designs, engineers and installs wall systems and doors for offices, has inked a new office and retail lease spanning 30,017 square feet at Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT)’s 1359 Broadway, the landlord announced Monday. “We are pleased to welcome Infinium to 1359 Broadway,” Ryan Kass, executive vice president, co-head of real estate and chief revenue officer at ESRT, said in a statement. “Innovative companies continue to seek well-located, modernized office space in New York City.” The asking rent and the length of the lease were not disclosed. The average asking rent for office space in Midtown was $84.99 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to Colliers research. Sarah Pontius, Jerica Lam, Carleigh Bettiol and Charlotte Reaman from Artisan Alliance represented Infinium in the deal. Jordan Berger, Shanae Ursini, and Kerry Lavelle represented ESRT in-house, along with Robert Lowe, Ron Lo Russo, Anthony LoPresti and Dan Organ from Cushman & Wakefield. Spokespeople for the brokerages did not immediately respond to requests for comment. “We evaluated numerous locations throughout Midtown, and 1359 Broadway consistently stood out,” Shawn Gaffney, founder of Infinium Wall Systems, told Commercial Observer. “Beyond the quality of the building itself, ESRT’s commitment to sustainability, long-term ownership, and partnership aligned closely with our own values. The southern exposure, private entrance, outdoor terrace, and opportunity to create a truly differentiated space made it the clear choice for our New York flagship.” This deal represents an expansion for Infinium, which also has an office and showroom address at 44 East 32nd Street. Built in 1924, 1359 Broadway is a 22-story office property with other tenants such as artificial intelligence company Scaled Cognition and nonprofit Braven. Amanda Schi
bullets: Infinium Wall Systems leased 30,017 SF office/retail at ESRT's 1359 Broadway in Midtown Manhattan.
Asking rent ~$85/SF average in Midtown Q2 2026; recent deals in building secured rents to $120/SF.
Midtown office leasing resurgence unrelated to multifamily residential demand or supply drivers.
stories.summary: Infinium Wall Systems, a designer and installer of wall and door systems, leased 30,017 square feet of office and retail space at Empire State Realty Trust's 1359 Broadway in Midtown Manhattan. Recent transactions in the 44-story tower, including deals with Coalesce Capital and Lionheart Strategic Management, have secured asking rents up to $120 per square foot. Average asking rent in Midtown during Q2 2026 was $84.99 per SF.
stories.impact: Midtown Manhattan office leasing velocity and rent growth ($84–$120/SF range) reflect tech and capital-markets tenant demand, not residential fundamentals. No direct multifamily relevance; however, office-to-residential conversion potential in Midtown towers remains a longer-term supply lever if office fundamentals weaken.

stories.title: Resource Realty Negotiates Warehouse Lease for Amazing Savings
stories.excerpt: Resource Realty of Northern New Jersey (RRNNJ) negotiated a 173,475-square-foot, long-term industrial lease at the newly constructed Spring Brook Commerce Center, located at 128 Iron Mountain Rd. in Mine Hill, NJ, on behalf of closeout retailer Amazing Savings. The transaction was led by the RRNNJ executive brokerage team of principals Tom Consiglio and Scott Peck, who represented Amazing Savings. The landlord is Brookfield Properties, represented by Lee & Associates’ Drew Maffey along with Rick Marchisio, Will Ziegler, Joseph Komitor and Matt Esposito. For more than 20 years, RRNNJ has served as the exclusive real estate advisor to Amazing Savings, navigating the retailer’s regional footprint expansion through multiple market cycles. Under the current leadership of Amazing Savings President Sam Friedland, the relationship continues to thrive. “Working with Tom Consiglio and the Resource Realty NNJ team is always an exceptional experience,” said Friedland. “Our relationship spans many years, and they have delivered for us once again. Tom’s deep market expertise shines through in every transaction.” The post Resource Realty Negotiates Warehouse Lease for Amazing Savings appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Amazing Savings leased 173,475 SF at newly constructed Spring Brook Commerce Center in Mine Hill, NJ.
Brookfield Properties landlord; closeout retailer footprint expansion via long-term warehouse lease.
Retail warehouse leasing in NJ unrelated to multifamily demand, supply, or capital markets.
stories.summary: Resource Realty of Northern New Jersey negotiated a long-term industrial lease of 173,475 square feet at Spring Brook Commerce Center in Mine Hill, NJ, on behalf of closeout retailer Amazing Savings. Brookfield Properties is the landlord. The transaction continues a 20-year relationship between RRNNJ and Amazing Savings managing the retailer's regional footprint expansion.
stories.impact: Industrial warehouse leasing activity in Mine Hill has no bearing on multifamily development, demand, or capital allocation. Logistics and closeout retail real estate operate in separate capital and geographic markets from residential development.

stories.title: Prologis Promotes Travis Harvey to Global Head of Development
stories.excerpt: Prologis has promoted Travis Harvey to lead the firm’s global development and construction platform across the Americas, Asia and Europe, Commercial Observer has learned. As global head of development, Harvey will use his more than 20 years of experience in the industry — including 11 years at Prologis — to strengthen the industrial giant’s platform. He will replace Greg Bauer, who recently retired from Prologis after 30 years with the company. “I’m grateful for the opportunity to build on the legacy Greg helped shape. Development is central to how we serve our customers, support the communities where we build and deliver value to investors,” Harvey said in a statement. “My focus will be on strengthening our platform, deepening our use of technology and data, and continuing to support the talented teams that bring our projects to life around the world.” Before joining Prologis, Harvey was vice president of land development at Flagler Global Logistics, where he worked on developing “strategic land assets” in Florida, according to Prologis. Replacing Harvey in his old position as senior vice president and head of development management for Prologis’ U.S. East region is Sheila Sutton, who was previously the firm’s vice president and development officer for the New York and New Jersey markets. Sheila Sutton. PHOTO: Courtesy Prologis Sutton joined Prologis in 2022 from Duke Realty, where she was vice president of construction operations and managed multiple projects throughout New Jersey and Pennsylvania. Prologis acquired Duke Realty in October 2022. “Prologis has established a track record in the East Region for delivering innovative, high-quality logistics projects in some of the most important hubs in the global supply chain, from Toronto down to Miami,” Sutton said in a statement. “I’m excited to build on that momentum with our teams, customers and local partners as we continue to deliver projects that support local economies and communities.” Both Harvey and Sutton
bullets: Prologis promoted Travis Harvey to global head of development across Americas, Asia, Europe.
Harvey succeeds retiring Greg Bauer after 30 years; prior experience in Florida land development.
Industrial/logistics leadership change unrelated to multifamily market fundamentals or capital flows.
stories.summary: Prologis promoted Travis Harvey to global head of development and construction platform across the Americas, Asia, and Europe, replacing Greg Bauer who retired after 30 years. Harvey brings more than 20 years of industry experience, including 11 years at Prologis and prior roles in Florida land development at Flagler Global Logistics.
stories.impact: Industrial REIT leadership changes have no direct impact on multifamily supply, demand, capital, or cost. This is an internal organizational move at a logistics-focused developer and does not signal shifts in real estate fundamentals or capital allocation toward residential.

stories.title: Infinity, KB Realty Partners Bag 75 Kenmare Street’s Retail Condo for $12M
stories.excerpt: A joint venture went shopping near SoHo and didn’t come away empty-handed. Infinity Real Estate and KB Realty Partners just acquired the retail condominium at 75 Kenmare Street in Manhattan’s Nolita neighborhood—just outside of SoHo — for $11.75 million, Commercial Observer can first report. The transaction, which closed July 16, adds to Infinity’s continued push into SoHo retail, and KB Realty’s expanding portfolio in the neighborhood via both retail and mixed-use investments. 75 Kenmare Street sits in a prime position at the corner of Kenmare and Mulberry streets. Its 10,000-square-foot retail component comprises two commercial units that are fully leased to tenants Undefeated, Nemesis Coffee, Bondi Sushi and M Jewelers. Atop the retail sits a luxury condo component — not included in the purchase — with 38 residential units designed by none other than Lenny Kravitz. First Atlantic Realty was the seller, and was advised by Masonre in the deal. ConnectOne Bank provided Infinity and KB Realty with acquisition financing. “Manhattan retail is where Infinity got its start nearly 20 years ago, and this acquisition marks a deliberate return to that core focus,” Steven Kassin, a managing partner at Infinity Real Estate, said in a statement. “75 Kenmare Street is an attractive asset to continue growing our footprint in Manhattan retail.” Kassin founded Infinity Real Estate in 2005. The firm — which develops, owns and manages real estate across the U.S. — is headquartered in New York, but also has an office in Miami today. “This is exactly the kind of asset we look for,” David Berg, a partner at Infinity Real Estate, said. “Strong in-place tenancy, dependable cash flow from day one, and a tenant mix that genuinely fits the neighborhood.” The deal follows Infinity Real Estate’s acquisition of 40 Bleecker Street in late 2024. The firm paid $13.3 million to Broad Street Development and Crow Holdings for the similarly sized retail condominium, also
bullets: Infinity Real Estate and KB Realty Partners acquired retail condo at 75 Kenmare Street, SoHo/Nolita, for $11.75M.
10,000 SF retail (2 units fully leased; Undefeated, Nemesis, Bondi Sushi, M Jewelers); 38 luxury condo units above (Kravitz-designed).
SoHo/Nolita retail acquisition signals tight urban retail scarcity; multifamily upside in trophy Manhattan neighborhood zoning.
stories.summary: Infinity Real Estate and KB Realty Partners acquired the retail condominium at 75 Kenmare Street in Manhattan's Nolita neighborhood (just outside SoHo) for $11.75 million. The property includes 10,000 square feet of retail space comprising two fully-leased units and sits atop 38 luxury residential units designed by Lenny Kravitz. The transaction closed July 16 and expands both partners' portfolios in the SoHo/Nolita area.
stories.impact: Purchase of trophy SoHo/Nolita retail condo signals scarcity value and institutional appetite for mixed-use assets in constrained Manhattan submarkets. While this deal is retail-focused, it reflects broader SoHo/Nolita density and land value strength; developers evaluating residential or mixed-use development in Nolita should expect continued competition for limited sites and upward pressure on land costs in this neighborhood.

stories.title: Law Firm Hodgson Russ Renews 20K SF at Fisher Brothers’ 605 Third Avenue
stories.excerpt: Law firm Hodgson Russ spearheaded a rush of new leases at Fisher Brothers’ 605 Third Avenue. The firm renewed its 19,900-square-foot lease on the 23rd floor of the tower, located between East 39th and East 40th streets, in June, according to the landlord. Newmark’s Brian Cohen and Kyle Ciminelli negotiated the 10-year renewal on behalf of the law firm. Several deals inked in the building since April secured rents up to $120 per square foot, according to Fisher Brothers. The landlord, represented in-house by Clark Briffel, did not disclose exact prices. Other recent activity in the 44-story building included private equity firm Coalesce Capital’s 16,900-square-foot renewal and expansion in June. The deal secured Coalesce its home on the 36th floor for the next five years. Meanwhile, investment firm Lionheart Strategic Management also expanded in June, taking 6,400 additional square feet on the 16th floor for 10 years. Lionheart’s total footprint at the property following the new expansion was unclear. Both firms were represented in-house in the lease deals. These busy tenants can expect to remain caffeinated for the next decade, at least, as ground-floor retail tenant Starbucks signed a 10-year renewal across 1,800 square feet in July. David Firestein of TSCG negotiated on behalf of the coffee chain. A 130,000-square-foot block of availability on 605 Third Avenue’s fourth, fifth and sixth floors remains, according to the Fisher Brothers. The landlord has retained a CBRE team of Howard Fiddle, Peter Turchin, Ben Joseph, Brett Shannon, Max Tarter, Zachary Price and Natalie Chung to market the spaces. Spokespeople for Newmark and the tenants did not immediately respond to requests for comment. TSCG could not be reached for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Hodgson Russ law firm renewed 19,900 SF lease at Fisher Brothers' 605 Third Avenue, Midtown Manhattan (10 years).
Recent deals in 44-story tower secured rents up to $120/SF; other tenants (Coalesce, Lionheart) renewed/expanded.
Midtown office leasing surge; no direct multifamily demand or capital signal.
stories.summary: Law firm Hodgson Russ renewed its 19,900-square-foot lease on the 23rd floor of Fisher Brothers' 605 Third Avenue in Midtown Manhattan for 10 years. Several deals inked in the building since April secured rents up to $120 per square foot. Other notable activity includes private equity firm Coalesce Capital's 16,900 SF renewal and expansion in June, and Lionheart Strategic Management's 6,400 SF expansion.
stories.impact: Midtown office renewal activity and rising rents ($120/SF) reflect professional services and capital-markets demand, not residential multifamily fundamentals. No direct impact on multifamily development. Longer-term: sustained office strength reduces conversion potential as a residential supply lever.

stories.title: Stetson University plans Tampa development as higher ed bets on mixed-use districts
stories.excerpt: Stetson University is joining the higher education trend of investing in mixed-use districts, with the redevelopment of its Tampa Law Center. Stetson is partnering with New York-based Bromley Companies on plans to develop 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37,500 square feet of retail space and 440,000 square feet of office space, along with a new law center, on the 6.5-acre site in the Tampa Heights district, according to ConnectCRE.  Stetson University acquired the property from the City of Tampa in 2002, according to the Tampa Business Journal. It was previously the site of the Tampa Heights police station.  The university’s […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Stetson University partnering with Bromley Companies on 6.5-acre Tampa Heights mixed-use redevelopment.
Plan: 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37.5K SF retail, 440K SF office, new law center; rezoning required; phased construction starting late 2025.
Higher-ed mixed-use trend (USF Fletcher District model) signals institutional capital targeting residential+office+hotel portfolios in secondary markets.
stories.summary: Stetson University is partnering with New York-based Bromley Companies to redevelop its 6.5-acre Tampa Law Center into a mixed-use district. The project includes 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37,500 square feet of retail, and 440,000 square feet of office space, along with a new law school facility. The development requires rezoning approval and is expected to begin construction in late 2025. The university's 1,000 law students will anchor demand.
stories.impact: Stetson-Bromley partnership is part of a national higher-ed real estate trend (see USF's $268M Fletcher District) to monetize campuses and create student villages via mixed-use development. This signals strong institutional capital appetite and zoning receptivity for large residential projects in secondary markets like Tampa. For multifamily developers: the competition for land and construction labor in Tampa will intensify; the 670-unit phased delivery will absorb labor and local financing capacity. Stetson's student anchor suggests counter-cyclical demand resilience, supporting rents in Tampa Heights even if broader multifamily softens.

stories.title: Fraud scheme: real estate journalism’s version of “if it bleeds, it leads”
stories.excerpt: Real estate’s version of “if it bleeds, it leads” usually includes some version of the phrase “fraud scheme.” DFW real estate’s latest 15-car pileup on I-35 is the case of two Frisco entrepreneurs who already had a go-round with allegations of a “Ponzi scheme” on their track record before concocting promotional materials for a luxury waterfront development on the San Gabriel River that they never intended to build, the Dallas Morning News reported. Promotional materials for the 400-acre development showed a mixed-use development with office retail, restaurants, parks and a canal big enough for boats on the Central Texas river […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder (Frisco entrepreneurs) ordered to pay $400M damages for fake San Gabriel River development.
Breach of contract/fraud; $300M punitive damages; earlier SEC restraining order for alleged $130M Ponzi scheme (2023).
Fraud case is market scandal, not a structural market signal; limited impact on DFW multifamily development outlook.
stories.summary: A Williamson County jury found Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder liable for breach of contract and fraud related to a fake 400-acre mixed-use development on the San Gabriel River near Dripping Springs, Texas. The defendants were ordered to pay $400 million in damages, including $300 million in punitive damages, to Energy Commissioning Inc. for infrastructure work on a project that never materialized. The pair had also faced SEC action in 2023 for an alleged $130 million Ponzi scheme targeting DFW's Indian-American community.
stories.impact: This is a high-profile fraud case affecting specific entrepreneurs and their financiers; it does not signal structural changes in DFW multifamily demand, supply, capital, or cost. The reputational damage may tighten underwriting for developers with ties to the accused, but no systemic market impact on development feasibility or capital availability for legitimate projects in the Dallas-Fort Worth metro.

stories.title: Align ups unit count, lowers height for controversial Marina Safeway project
stories.excerpt: Align Real Estate is making its contentious Marina Safeway redevelopment a little shorter and a little denser, revising its proposal as it tries to steer one of San Francisco’s most closely watched housing projects through city approval. The San Francisco-based developer submitted updated plans that would reduce the project’s tallest towers from 25 and 22 stories to 22 and 18 stories while increasing the residential count from 790 to 848 units, the San Francisco Chronicle reported. The redesign trims roughly 40 feet from the east tower and 23 feet from the west tower with the entire development at 15 Marina […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Align Real Estate revised Marina Safeway redevelopment: towers reduced to 22 & 18 stories; units increased 790→848 (58 net adds).
989.5K SF total; 15 Marina Boulevard SF waterfront; Assembly Bill 2011 streamlined approval path; Mayor Lurie opposition continues.
SF housing debate and waterfront zoning approvals now favor density over height; approval risk remains high for large projects.
stories.summary: Align Real Estate revised its contentious Marina Safeway redevelopment proposal to reduce the tallest towers from 25 and 22 stories to 22 and 18 stories while increasing residential units from 790 to 848 (net +58 units). The 989,500-square-foot project at 15 Marina Boulevard is being reviewed under California's Assembly Bill 2011, which streamlines approvals for qualifying housing on commercial land. Mayor Daniel Lurie remains opposed to the project despite the revisions, though the Planning Department continues review.
stories.impact: Align's density-over-height compromise on SF waterfront reflects AB 2011's push for residential unit maximization on commercial sites, but persistent mayoral and neighborhood opposition signals extended approval timelines and regulatory uncertainty. For multifamily developers in SF: AB 2011 creates a legal path to bypass local opposition on commercial sites, but political headwinds remain real. Capital and construction financing may tighten if approvals stall; the Marina project's difficulties suggest SF waterfront and trophy submarket projects face 18–36 month approval cycles and heightened community resistance, impacting development feasibility and hold costs.

stories.title: Miami design board swoons over OKO Lilli, 13th Floor’s Nobu condo towers
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s second condo tower in Edgewater and 13th Floor Investments’ proposal for a Nobu-branded residential building next to a historic Brickell church are advancing.  The Miami Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of both developments on Wednesday, with most board members gushing over the designs of what would be the latest luxury tower additions to the city’s quickly evolving skyline. One board member voted no on Doronin’s planned building, citing issues with some of the design.  Miami-based OKO Group, led by Doronin, plans the 53-story, 117-unit project, called OKO Lilli Tower, on the half-acre lot at 717 Northeast […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami UDRB approved OKO Lilli (Vlad Doronin): 53-story, 117-unit tower + 8-story parking garage, 717 NE 27th Street, Edgewater.
Also approved: 13th Floor's 75-story, 321-unit Nobu-branded tower at 619 Brickell Ave. (Arnaud Karsenti lead).
Luxury condo approvals in Edgewater/Brickell signal strong institutional appetite for high-rise residential in Miami CBD/waterfront.
stories.summary: The Miami Urban Development Review Board voted to approve two luxury residential projects: OKO Group's 53-story, 117-unit OKO Lilli Tower in Edgewater (designed by Adrian Smith + Gordon Gill) and 13th Floor's 75-story, 321-unit tower branded by Nobu at 619 Brickell Avenue. Both projects advance Miami's luxury condo development pipeline in prime waterfront and downtown locations. UDRB approval is advisory; final authority rests with the city's planning director.
stories.impact: UDRB approval of two major luxury residential towers (438 combined units) in prime Miami submarkets (Edgewater/Brickell) signals strong institutional capital confidence and zoning receptivity for high-rise residential. For multifamily developers: this underscores Miami's continued role as a condo/high-end rental market, not workforce or middle-market multifamily. Competitive intensity for trophy waterfront and CBD sites will remain elevated, driving land costs and construction prices. Approval timelines appear manageable (UDRB cleared both); developers should expect 12–18 month permitting for well-capitalized, design-quality projects in Miami's primary markets.

stories.title: Palm Beach County nixes proposed Project Tango data center
stories.excerpt: Plans for a proposed data center in Palm Beach County hit a major roadblock Wednesday after county commissioners rejected a zoning application that would have allowed the project’s development.  At the end of a 12-hour hearing, officials voted 5-1 to deny an amended zoning application that would have permitted for a 1 million-square-foot data center at 20125 State Road 80/Southern Boulevard, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The project also would have included 2.3 million square feet of warehouses and 216,000 square feet for minor utility use.  Plans for Central Park Commerce Center, also known as Project Tango, were filed […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Palm Beach County commissioners voted 5-1 to deny amended zoning for Project Tango data center (1M SF) at State Road 80.
PBA Holdings' application rejected over infrastructure and community impact concerns; 80+ speakers opposed; motion without prejudice.
Community opposition halted logistics/data-center supply; no direct multifamily impact; signals zoning/political resistance to large-format development.
stories.summary: Palm Beach County commissioners voted 5-1 to deny an amended zoning application for Project Tango (Central Park Commerce Center), a proposed 1 million-square-foot data center plus 2.3 million square feet of warehouses at State Road 80/Southern Boulevard. The decision followed a 12-hour hearing with over 80 public speakers opposing the project, citing concerns about infrastructure impact and community effects. The motion was made without prejudice, allowing resubmission.
stories.impact: Rejection of large-format logistics/data center zoning in Palm Beach County reflects community and infrastructure pushback on industrial development in residential areas. While not a multifamily project, this outcome signals heightened political risk and extended approval timelines for large-footprint development in South Florida suburban markets. Multifamily developers in Palm Beach County should expect similar community scrutiny and zoning/infrastructure concerns; land values in mixed-use or residential-zoned sites may command premiums over industrial-zoned parcels subject to approval risk.

stories.title: Musk’s companies prep $5M Seaholm office renovation
stories.excerpt: Downtown Austin’s Seaholm Power Plant, which was quietly occupied by several Elon Musk-affiliated companies earlier this year, is getting a $5.2 million facelift.  The planned renovations have been put in place by Wit Tech, a company with links to SpaceX, the firm that is pushing the construction of the Terafab semiconductor manufacturing plant in Grimes County. Musk’s companies occupy 35,000 square feet of space at 800 West Cesar Chavez Street, according to the Austin American Statesman. Musk is typically bombastic about his companies’ pursuits and ambitious, but has not yet publicly commented on the office space. He has given notes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Musk-affiliated companies (via Wit Tech/SpaceX) undertaking $5.2M office renovation at Seaholm Power Plant, Austin.
35,000 SF occupied at 800 West Cesar Chavez; Terafab semiconductor project (55B+, 100M SF potential) reshaping Grimes County (east Austin).
Major employer (Musk/Tesla/SpaceX) consolidation in Austin metro signals long-term job/population growth; multifamily demand upstream in East County.
stories.summary: Elon Musk-affiliated companies, including SpaceX-linked Wit Tech, are investing $5.2 million to renovate office space at Seaholm Power Plant (800 West Cesar Chavez) in downtown Austin, where they occupy 35,000 square feet. This follows Musk's acquisition of 6,000+ acres in Grimes County (east of Austin) for the Terafab semiconductor manufacturing project, which could reach $55 billion in investment and up to 100 million square feet of development. Grimes County commissioners approved tax breaks and a tax increment reinvestment zone.
stories.impact: Musk's consolidated Austin-area footprint (downtown office + massive Grimes County mega-fab) signals a major inbound employer concentration that will drive material job and population migration to Austin metro and eastern growth corridors over the next 5–10 years. For multifamily developers: this is a high-conviction demand signal; begin acquiring land in Grimes County and east Austin submarkets near Terafab now, before land values and construction costs inflate with certainty of project delivery. The Terafab project will create thousands of construction and permanent jobs, anchoring long-term rental demand. Early-stage developers will benefit from land-cost arbitrage and development-feasibility upside as infrastructure and labor costs respond to Musk's announced 100M SF vision.
